3.0 Scope and Purpose The scope of this procedure is to describe the processes used in Operation Program Management to communicate , highlight and influence the different Programs stakeholders ( Marketing , Engineering , Operations Business Units and Aftermarket ) . OPM 's purpose is to ensure we deliver a producible product that is sourced to meet program objectives of cost , yield , quality and schedule . 6.0 Operations Program Management Communication Flow OPM request Marketing , Engineering , Operations Business Units and Aftermarket to provide information about program status . This allows the OPE / OPA to identify operational risks , establish strategies and ensure the execution of risk mitigation plans to achieve customer satisfaction . The figure below shows the communication flow between Operations and different Program stakeholders . This model serves also as an escalation process for the Top Operations / Program issues . Page : 5 of 6 Program Management 6.1 Operations Reviews 6.1.1 Key Program Update ( KPU ) Weekly review owned by the OPM Executive Director or Vice President , which summarizes in one or two slides the key program updates . The presenters are the OPM Managers or their delegate . The attendance is composed of all Operations Executives ( Supply Management & Manufacturing ) and STOA . The goal of this review is to highlight short and medium term Program issues , to show progress of the risk mitigation plans and to request the adequate support and priority if necessary . 6.1.2 Program Customer Operations Review ( PCOR ) Quarterly review owned by the OPM Executive Director , which provides the Program status , directions , and strategies . The attendance is composed of Operations Business Units management , STOA , SI & OP , and PMT members ( Marketing , Aftermarket , Program Engineering & CQM ) . The goal of this review is to highlight the top ten medium and long term issues in order to be able to assess the ability to meet customer demand over a horizon of two years and , also , all the Program strategies . Page : 6 of 6 Program Management Refer to OPMWI 1.10.1 Manage and Prepare PCOR for details . 6.1.3 Program Clinic Bi-weekly review owned by OPM . The attendance is SM , IMF , Program Engineering and STOA Engineering . The goal of this review is to ensure a progression of the top twenty ( 20 ) risks in terms of OTD , quality and cost to make sure we have an on-going plans to address these issues . It is an extension of the PCORs , but at a more tactical level . The OPM Risk Cube is a tool that may be used to establish the agenda . Refer to Program Clinic Cookbook for details . 6.2 Program Reviews 6.2.1 Thursday Leadership Meetings ( TLM ) TLM are held according to a calendar defined by Marketing . It presents the following : - Market overview ; - Program strategies ; - OEM scorecard ; - Quality scorecard for

Risk Cube is a tool that may be used to establish the agenda . Refer to Program Clinic Cookbook for details . 6.2 Program Reviews 6.2.1 Thursday Leadership Meetings ( TLM ) TLM are held according to a calendar defined by Marketing . It presents the following : - Market overview ; - Program strategies ; - OEM scorecard ; - Quality scorecard for interventions at OEM or events in the field ; - Upcoming Engineering Changes to improve product or services ; - Program financial results ( i.e . FSC , ROS , etc ) ; - Other program issues . Open issues or questions are monitored via an action list if required . The attendance is all the Team 1 members ( President & Vice Presidents ) and all the PMMT members or their delegates . OPM provides and communicate as the Operations PMMT team member : - OTD of engines to customer commitment ; - Recovery plans if required ; - Industrialization plan for specific Engineering Changes ; Page : 7 of 6 Program Management - All other Operations support material or information required . 6.2.2 PMMT ( Top ≈10 risks ) Meeting held on an as needed basis ( monthly basis ideally ) led by Customer Service ( Legacy ) or Program Engineering ( NPI ) Director . The attendance is the senior management of Marketing , Program Engineering , Customer Service , OPM and CQM . It manages and directs a specific product across all functions and disciplines . A program oriented team approach is utilized to integrate all functions and disciplines into the program planning and decision making process and the management of risk . Please refer to MMP219 . 6.2.3 PMT ( Top ≈20 risks ) PMT reviews are held bi-weekly ( or as required ) and are owned by the Customer Service for Legacy programs or Program Engineering for NPI programs . The attendance is Program Manager , Project Engineer , Customer Quality Manager , Marketing Account Manager , CM , OPM and Customer Service representative . The goal of this review is to ensure a progression of the Customer/Program top risks and make sure we have an on-going plan to address these issues . It is an extension of the PMMT . 6.2.4 Integrated Product Team ( IPT ) IPTs are formed to manage introduction of complex Engineering Changes . Usually OPM is invited when the substantiation and industrialization plan is complex , when Risk Hardware will be needed , or when the incorporation needs to occur in a short timeframe . This meeting is usually attended by Program Engineering , Customer Service , Supply Management , Produceability , OPM , Configuration Management . Refer to IPT Review Form 6386 for Minutes of meeting format . 7.0 Other Activities OPM Manager / OPE / OPA may be resquested to provide data for other presentations such as : Ops . Introduction The purpose of this cookbook is to explain how to manage Program Customer Operations Review ( PCOR ) owned by the OPM Sr . Manager . It provides program status , directions , and strategies . The attendance is composed of Marketing , CQM , STOA , SI & OP , PMT and all Operations Business Units top offenders . The goal of this review is to highlight the top ten medium and long term issues in order to be able to assess the ability to meet customer demand over a horizon of two years and , also , all the Program strategies . When a new program is starting , the PCOR is an opportunity to address the Operations Business Directors to present the new program with a dedicated NPI PCOR . PCOR presentation preparation is done by the Operations Program Engineer ( OPE ) or Operations Program Analyst ( OPA ) with the support of OPM Management when necessary . The PCOR checklist is found on the OPM website . Logistics Section The PCOR meetings invitation ( including room reservation ) is sent by the OPM Sr. Manager ’ s Administrative Assistant . Usually the meetings are scheduled on Mondays for the year ahead and occur approximately every 3 months per program . OPM Sr . Manager sends the invitation to a core distribution list that remains the same for all PCORs , it includes VP , BU Managers , Engineering and Quality . For OPM , the process starts at least three weeks ahead of PCOR presentation with a discussion between OPM Manager , OPE / OPA and Planner to identify Top Program Issues ( mid to long term ) . Usually the previous PCOR slides are used to initiate the meeting , and from that discussion , an agenda is prepared which also identifies the presenters and / or slide owners . The OPE / OPA is responsible to add to the main invitation , the slide presenters / owners that will present the Top Program Issues . This is done by forwarding the invitation for the PCOR in Outlook . Has no buisiness continuity plan Single Point Of Failure A single point of failure ( SPOF ) is an element or a part of a system for which no backup esixts and that the failure of witch will disable the entire system . Three elements may constitute a source for SPOF : Capability , Capacity and failure within . SPOFs are undesirable to systems requiring reliability and availability of the supply chain . The main objective is to secure the program supply chain any SPOF risks must be addressed through a robust mitigation plan , short term and long term . A robust Single point of failure mitigation plan is in place Single point of failure mitigation plan is defined and on track to meet target completion date Single point of failure analysis is in progress . Some mitigation plans are still not defined Business Continuity Plan A business continuity plan enables critical parts to be continually delivered . Instead of focusing on resuming a business after critical operations have ceased , or recovering after a disaster , a business continuity plan endeavors to ensure that critical operations continue to be available . Business Continuity Planning is a proactive planning process that ensures critical services or products are delivered during a disruption . A Business Continuity Plan includes : ■Plans , measures and arrangements to ensure the continuous delivery of critical services and products , which permits the organization to recover its facility , data and assets . ■Identification of necessary resources to support business continuity , including personnel , information , equipment , financial allocations , legal counsel , infrastructure protection and accommodations . Controlled conditions of product or service definition include , as applicable : a ) The availability of documents that describes the characteristics of the product to be produced , the services to be provided , or the activities to be performed and the results to be achieved . Note 1 : Definition documents can include digital product definition data , drawings , parts lists , materials and process specifications . Note 2 : Definition documents for activities to be performed and results to be achieved can include process flow charts , control plans , production documents ( e.g. , manufacturing plans , travelers , routers , work orders , process cards ) , and verification documents . b ) The availability of work instructions , as necessary c ) The use of suitable infrastructure and environment for the operation of processes ; Note : Suitable infrastructure can include product specific tools ( e.g. , jigs , fixtures , molds ) and software programs . d ) The availability and use of monitoring and measuring equipment e ) The implementation of monitoring and measurement activities at appropriate stages to verify that criteria for control of processes or outputs , and acceptance criteria for products and services , have been met : To ensure that records for monitoring and measurement activity for product acceptance includes :  Criteria for acceptance and rejection ;  Where in the sequence verification operations are to be performed ;  Measurement results to be retained ( at a minimum an indication of acceptance or rejection ) ;  Any specific monitoring and measurement equipment required and instructions associated with their use ; To ensure that when sampling is used as a means of product acceptance , the sampling plan is justified on the basis of recognized statistical principles and appropriate for use ( i.e. , matching the sampling plan to the criticality of the product and to the process capability ) . f ) The implementation of product release , delivery and post-delivery activities g ) Accountability for all products during production ( e.g. , parts quantities , split orders , nonconforming product ) . h ) Evidence

of recognized statistical principles and appropriate for use ( i.e. , matching the sampling plan to the criticality of the product and to the process capability ) . f ) The implementation of product release , delivery and post-delivery activities g ) Accountability for all products during production ( e.g. , parts quantities , split orders , nonconforming product ) . h ) Evidence that all production and inspection/verification operations have been completed as planned , or as otherwise documented and authorized . i ) Control of Foreign Object Damage ( FOD ) per Pratt & Whitney Worldwide Procedure ( WP ) 5.3.5 j ) Storage requirements , including periodic preservation/condition checks , are defined for production equipment or tooling in storage k ) Monitoring and control of utilities and supplies ( e.g. , water , compressed air , electricity , and chemical products ) to the extent they affect conformity to product requirements . l ) Criteria for workmanship specified in the clearest practical way ( e.g. , written standards , representative samples , illustrations ) m ) Stipulating appropriate methods for authorizing receipt to , and dispatch from , designated storage areas P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 07-05 Quality Management System REV : 12 Procedure DATE : 12 September 2018 PAGE : 6 of 13 PRODUCTION AND SERVICE PROVISION n ) The appointment of competent persons , including any required qualification o ) The validation and revalidation , of the ability to achieve planned results of the processes for production and service provision , where the resulting output can not be verified by subsequent monitoring or measurement ; NOTE : These processes can be referred to as special processes p ) The implementation of actions to prevent human error ; q ) The identification and recording of products released for subsequent production use pending completion of all required measuring and monitoring activities , to allow recall and replacement if it is later found that the product does not meet requirements . 3.2.5 First Article Inspection ( FAI ) 3.2.5.1 Production operations and service provision shall use a representative item from the first production run of a new part or assembly to verify that the production process , production documentation and tooling are capable of producing parts and assemblies that meet requirements . This process shall be repeated when changes occur that invalidate the original results ( e.g. , engineering changes , manufacturing process changes , tooling changes , etc . ) Note : FAI at the engine level is accomplished via the Manufacturing Approval Process . 3.3 . Control of Production and Service Provision 3.3.1 . Production operations and service provision are carried out in accordance with approved data . 3.3.1.1 In accordance with MMP 508 , Process Approval Appointees ( PAAs ) are responsible for certain functions related to the approval and control of processes . The functions include :  Ensuring PPDP is followed  Verification that product and process meet product definition

and Service Provision 3.3.1 . Production operations and service provision are carried out in accordance with approved data . 3.3.1.1 In accordance with MMP 508 , Process Approval Appointees ( PAAs ) are responsible for certain functions related to the approval and control of processes . The functions include :  Ensuring PPDP is followed  Verification that product and process meet product definition  Validation of FAI/MOS and signature as IPT representative and for process approval 3.3.2 . Changes to production processes are reviewed by the Integrated Product Teams ( IPT ) through the PPDP/IPMP processes . Personnel authorized to approve changes to production processes are identified by each relevant business unit per MMP 219 . 3.3.3 . Each relevant business unit requiring a change to a production process identifies the need for the change to the Integrated Product Team ( IPT ) /Program Management Team ( PMT ) , ref . MMP219/MMP203 . The results of changes to production processes are assessed to confirm that the desired effect has been achieved without adverse effects to product conformity . 3.3.6 . Production equipment , tools and software programs used to automate and control/monitor product realization processes are validated prior to release for production and are maintained . 3.3.7 . Validate Special Processes 3.5.1 . Processes for production and service provision where the resulting output can not be verified by subsequent monitoring or measurement , and , as a consequence , deficiencies become apparent only after the product or the service is in use , are referred to as special processes . 3.5.2 . Each Relevant business units , as applicable , inspects customer property upon receipt for : a ) Handling or transportation damage b ) To ensure that package or container remains sealed prior to use , as applicable c ) Identification and quantity d ) Adequacy of the certification accompanying the customer property as required e ) General condition of the customer property to ensure that it conforms to the extent reported by the customer 3.7.4 . All customer property , whether in use , being processed , or in stores : a ) Is segregated b ) Is safeguarded from deterioration or damage caused by contamination and corrosion , mishandling , misuse or damage . . 1.3 This Quality Management System procedure ( QMSP ) applies to the entire Pratt & Whitney Canada ( P & WC ) Approved Manufacture Centre ( AMC ) as defined in the Quality Program Manual ( QPM ) including Global Operations & Strategy ( GO & S ) . 1.4 The processing of nonconforming material for PW1200G , PW1500G and PW1900 shall follow the requirements of the QMSP 08-03-1000 procedure . The PW800 engine model follows requirements defined in QMSP 08-03 series . 1.5 Partners are required to conform to the requirements of QMSP 08-03 series as are internal and external suppliers . In general , partners are the design authority for their hardware . As such , they may disposition minor deviations ( as defined by QPs , QMSP 08-03 series ) without P & WC approval . Major deviations must be declared to P & WC for approval . 1.6 For HSP ( Hamilton Sundstrand Poland ) : the process for handling nonconformities or variations of Auxiliary Power Units [ APUs ] from engineering test limits or customer contractual requirements , which are detected on production engines or modules , is outside the scope of this procedure . The process is defined under HSP procedure “ Production Organisation Exposition 2/DJS/POE ” . 2 . DEFINITIONS – ACRONYMS 2.1 Definitions Engineering Part Number Level : sometimes referred to “ finished part number level ” , or “ 3 Million part number level ” , or “ 3M ” part number level . It refers to the drawings that are controlled by Engineering . Escape : Raw material , parts , assemblies , engines and IPPS ( Integrated Power Plant System ) which do not meet engineering design intent ( including duplicate serial number ) , which may have been released for further processing for incorporation into a higher assembly , a new or maintenance engine or for spare orders . In process nonconformity : Nonconformity detected during manufacturing . Established Authorizations : An Authorized transaction , which was previously approved by Material Review MR and Quality Review ( QR ) , provides to inspection/ operations the authority to process subsequent parts exhibiting same nonconformity ( i.e . cut off with termination date ) . Nonconforming product : is a product that does not meet product definition , including Engineering Source Approval . Nature of the nonconformity : Major : A nonconformity to the requirements in a specification , drawing or other documentation forming part of the engineering definition of the material , that potentially affect fit , form or function , including performance , durability , interchangeability , application , serialization , component published life , imbedded software , certification basis , reliability , maintainability , effective use or operation , weight , appearance ( where a P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03-02 Quality Management System REV : 21 Procedure DATE : 14 September 2023 PAGE : 5 of 27 Nonconforming Product factor ) ,

REQUIREMENTS 3.1 Inspectors / qualified personnel are responsible to report any nonconformity reported at any part level ( casting , forging , machining , assembly ) on a QN . 3.2 A QN is used to identify deviations to requirements defined by approved data ( i.e . drawings , specifications ) . Modification to a product that takes a characteristic beyond the configuration defined by the approved data must be authorized via an accepted engineering change process and may not be dispositioned via a QN . 3.3 All nonconforming products within the manufacturing cycle at P & WC will be reported and dispositioned . The reporting vehicles of nonconformities are as follows : QN , RFEA , EQIR , EEM and RFA . 3.4 Nonconforming product accepted for P & WC requirements is documented and approved taking into consideration as example : safety , reliability , performance , interchangeability , maintainability , function or operation , life limitations , weight and visual indications . 3.4.1 All nonconforming product and documentation is to be positively identified individually with a label or tag with the applicable report number as a traceable reference number to prevent re-introduction of product in production/ spares ( where applicable electronically UI ’ s or SN ’ s may be used ) . An approved marker may also be used to identify the parts . If the product is dispositioned as acceptable , the label or tag markings are to be removed from the subject product . 3.4.2 In the case where it is more practical to identify the product as a lot , the lot shall be enclosed in a container , the container shall be identified with a label/ tag with the applicable report number as a traceable reference number . 3.5 When a QN is used on a specific part number and inspection lot to report nonconforming product , the subject part number and inspection lot shall not be routed to subsequent assemblies , stores and Engine Centres prior closure of the opened QN of the subject part number and inspection lot . Exceptions shall be handled by local Quality procedures ensuring that no parts with open QNs will be delivered / shipped to the end- customers . 3.6 QN must be completed in SAP as per QMSP 08-03-05 . When required , a QN standard texts print out is completed to control the routing of the nonconforming material in accordance with the nonconformity report . 6.4.2 Reworks to critical parts and to ENSIP critical parts are performed in accordance with MMP 213 and MMP 503 respectively . 6.5 Special Work Instruction Sheet ( SWIS ) / Canadian Pratt & Whitney ( CPW ) specification 6.5.1 The use of an engineering approved SWIS/CPW is authorized when nonconformity may be reworked on a critical part and on an ENSIP critical . QN shall then be raised if required . 6.6 Disposition : Product Repair within the Production Environment 6.6.1 A `` repair '' disposition indicates that nonconforming product is to be re-processed by the approved repair procedure ( QRIS ) , to restore it to an acceptable design equivalent . When required , an MQ Tag or equivalent traveller is completed to control the routing of the nonconforming material in accordance with the QRIS . Repairs to critical parts and to ENSIP critical parts are performed in accordance with MMP 213 and MMP 503 respectively . 6.6.2 AMC organizations and supplier may use Quality Repair Instruction Sheet ( QRIS ) as the approved repair procedure in cases of repairs on production parts foreseen to be recurring . This process is defined under QMSP 08-03-03 . 6.6.2.1 For cases where repairs on production parts are not foreseen to be recurring , AMC organizations use the repair procedure as defined on the nonconforming report . 6.6.2.2 For repairs required on development parts , AMC organizations normally use an EEM as per ETOI 4508-01 . Some repairs may be accomplished through the use of a QRIS when authorized by the responsible Project Engineer . 6.6.3 QRIS repair process per QMSP 08-03-03 must be used when additional material ( e.g . plasma spray , nickel plating , sleeve repair ) is required to complete the repair ( material not to original DWG requirements ) . 6.7 Disposition

PURPOSE / SCOPE 1.1 This procedure defines the Red Flag Notice ( RFN ) , which is a mean to contain and maintain traceability of nonconforming product that has escaped the producer ’ s quality system . 1.2 The decision for issuing an RFN is determined by the Escape Management Process as per QMSP 08-03-04 . 1.3 This procedure covers the issue , process and use of the Red Flag Notice . 1.4 This Quality Management System procedure ( QMSP ) applies to the Pratt & Whitney Canada ( P & WC ) Aircraft Powerplant Business ( APB ) as defined in the Quality Program Manual ( QPM ) . 2 . DEFINITIONS / ACRONIMS 2.1 Definitions None 2.2 Acronyms APB Aircraft Powerplant Business EOP Engineering Organization Procedure OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer OPM Operations Program Management QMSP Quality Management System Procedure QPM Quality Program Manual RCCA Root Cost Cause Analysis RFN Red Flag Notice S/C Source Control 3 . REQUIREMENTS 3.1 The need for an RFN is determined during the Escape Management Process . If there are affected parts at the OEM or if engine deliveries will be impacted , the following representatives must be involved in the decision process prior to issuing the RFN : Marketing , Customer Quality Manager , Project Engineering , and OPM . If these representatives are not present , the meeting is to be re-convened with a special invitation sent to the missing parties . If they do not attend the follow-up meeting , the decision to issue the RFN will be made be those present . 3.2 An RFN is issued by Quality Performance to address quality issues as determined in

, and OPM . If these representatives are not present , the meeting is to be re-convened with a special invitation sent to the missing parties . If they do not attend the follow-up meeting , the decision to issue the RFN will be made be those present . 3.2 An RFN is issued by Quality Performance to address quality issues as determined in QMSP 08-03-04 and by the Engineering organization to address design issues as per EOP 22 . 3.2.1 . Quality Performance will request Engineering to countersign RFN ’ s in cases where the nonconformity is acceptable per Engineering . 3.3 An RFN provides directives as applicable for : P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED Aircraft Powerplant Business QMSP : 08-03-04-01 A P B REV : 1 Quality Management DATE : 28 September 2009 System Procedure PAGE : 4 of 6 Procedure Title : Red Flag Notice 3.3.1 . Stopping in process , manufactured or procured product ( raw material , detail , part assemblies ) and preventing shipment of engines and spare parts . 3.3.2 . Recalling and re-inspecting all affected parts in the Aircraft Powerplant Business ( APB ) and if necessary , the disassembly and re-inspection of parts from engines via the responsible Quality Manager . 3.4 Operations Program Management ( OPM ) to coordinate all containment actions through the Escape Management Process . 3.5 Marketing and Customer Support to issue appropriate documentation to address , recall and/or re-inspect product at the OEM ’ s , S/C and in the field . 3.6 RFN requirements are also applicable to all part numbers processed or held in quarantine and experimental areas , whether or not intended for production use . 3.7 The distribution for a Red Flag Notice is made to all affected V.P ’ s. , Directors and Managers identified at the Red Flag meeting as well as all affected and actioned personnel . 3.8 An RFN may only be closed by Quality Performance when there is compliance with all the directives and corrective action implemented . At a minimum the RCCA has to be accepted and closed , all products have been contained as per RFN instructions and appropriate documentation has been issued for engines at OEM , S/C and in the field . PURPOSE 1.1 Propel a culture of true Root Cause , Corrective / Preventive action including Read-Across ; Driving down escapes through prevention while maintaining visibility on Key issues . Excel towards a proactive mindset by understanding and addressing systemic issues This procedure defines the requirements and responsibilities around the Escape Management Process 2 . SCOPE 2.1 The Escape Management Process is initiated to gather a multidisciplinary team , when a product has been identified as non-conforming and is beyond the scope of the re-inspection order process , in order to ;  Assess the impact of the escape on operation , hardware , engine and the applicable customer ,  Establish containment action , determine & validate root cause , and establish & verify corrective action ,  Perform read-across & preventative activity to eliminate systemic issues and the risk of similar future escape . 2.2 This Quality Management System procedure ( QMSP ) applies to Pratt & Whitney Canada ( P & WC ) Aircraft Powerplant Business ( APB ) as defined in the Quality Program Manual ( QPM ) . 3 . DEFINITIONS AND ACRONYMS 3.1 Definitions Escape : Raw material , parts , assemblies , engines , APU , and IPPS ( Integrated Power Plant System ) which do not meet specified requirement ( s ) , which were or may have been released for further processing for incorporation into a higher assembly , a new or maintenance engine or for spare orders , and meeting criteria listed under section 4 .

Raw material , parts , assemblies , engines , APU , and IPPS ( Integrated Power Plant System ) which do not meet specified requirement ( s ) , which were or may have been released for further processing for incorporation into a higher assembly , a new or maintenance engine or for spare orders , and meeting criteria listed under section 4 . Requirement ( s ) . Quality Escape : An escape where the component does not conform to the defined engineering definition Engineering Escape : An escape where the component conforms to the defined engineering definition but does no meet the design intent Produceability Escape : An escape on a component that is not yet transferred per WIM808 & /or SMQ- WI-007-A Escape Management Minutes ( EMM ) : The EMM contains all the relevant information and elements required to ensure that the escape is properly resolved The latest revision of P & WC form 10254 Nonconforming product : Is a product that does not meet product definition , including Engineering Source Approval . Potential Escape Meeting : Initial investigation meeting ( s ) to inform key stakeholders of the nonconformity event , to determine if the escape criteria , as defined in paragraph 4.2 is met , and warrant the initiation of the Escape Management Process . in accordance with these requirements . 4.2 The Escape Management Minutes defines all required information and activities within the following phases Investigation : Problem Description , Defect Characterization & Engineering Assessment Containment : Containment Plan & Customer Communication Recovery Plan : Plan Execution , Timeline & Customer Update Root Cause : Validate and Verify Root Cause Corrective Action : Validate and Verify Corrective Action Read-Across & Preventive Action : PFMEA , PPAP , Control Plan , MOS , QMS… Systemic Action Review P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03-04 Quality Management System REV : 7 Procedure DATE : 2 October 2023 PAGE : 6 of 13 Escape Management Process 4.3 Escape Management Criteria EQP escapes are escapes raised and managed in PLM . Within PLM , EQPs are divided into two types , QP=Yes and QP=No . ( ref . Appendix A ) The criteria to have a QP=Yes escape raised and tracked by Quality Performance , as part of the Escape Management Process , includes but is not limited to : a ) Nonconforming material released to OEM/Field/Spare parts which requires an engineering assessment for subsequent actions b ) Repeat escape as the result of ineffective containment/corrective action c ) Engine return from OEM or field as a result of a process/product issue d ) Recurrence of OEM interventions ( Strike 3 and above exihibiting same symptoms ) e ) RFN required f ) ESA approved process not followed per CPW 135 requirements g ) Escape activity mandated by SMS h ) Line stop at engine center or OEM due to quality or engineering issue impacting engine or aircraft delivery . i ) Customer Service SCN Category 1 and Category 2 escapes 4.3.1 . QP=No The criteria to have a QP=No escape raised and tracked by the responsible Business Unit , as part of the Escape Management Process , must fulfil the following three conditions : a ) An escape which does not impact fit and form b ) An escape for which the nonconforming material released to OEM/Field/Spare parts with an engineering assessment that must result in “ as-is ” with no subsequent actions c ) An escape which does not have critical rotating parts ( CRP ) and ENSIP parts ( Engine Structural Integrity Program ) Additionally , QP=No can be used to investigate in the following conditions but not limited to : I . Line stop at engine center not impacting engine delivery II . External or internal escape potentially meeting EQP escape criteria which require further investigation QP=No escapes can also be raised by Business Units to manage and record investigations not meeting the EQP criteria . 4.4 Escape Categories 4.4.1 Escape Category Definitions 4.4.1.1 Category 1 ( Safety Issue ) P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03-04 Quality Management System REV : 7 Procedure DATE : 2 October 2023 PAGE : 7 of 13 Escape Management Process A

record investigations not meeting the EQP criteria . 4.4 Escape Categories 4.4.1 Escape Category Definitions 4.4.1.1 Category 1 ( Safety Issue ) P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03-04 Quality Management System REV : 7 Procedure DATE : 2 October 2023 PAGE : 7 of 13 Escape Management Process A Category 1 escape is an event where the aircraft either fails to safely continue its flight or is exposed to a situation that significantly jeopardizes the ability of the aircraft to safely continue its flight . ( Ref , per CSOP P- 20 ) 4.4.1.2 Category 2 ( Major Customer Impact ) A Category 2 escape is , but not limited to : a ) An event in which the margin of safety is significantly reduced during the course of a flight , either at OEM or in the field , per CSOP P-20 definition ; b ) An event leading to application of flight restriction outside the published engine operating standards ; c ) An event where an OEM has missed promised delivery of a customer-owned aircraft/engine ; d ) An event where major disruptions of the workflow occurs at the customer resulting in engine removal and return e ) An event where non-conforming material was dispositioned as not meeting the engineering design intent , with severe limitations to the product definition , that may affect safe operation of engine and/or aircraft that result in field actions such as service bulletin of compliance code of 1 to 3 . ( Ref CSOP D- 29 ) f ) A Manufacturing or technical problem , or administrative error with a spare part sold by P & WC which results in an impact on the operator or engine maintenance shop such as missed promised delivery of a customer-owned engine from an engine maintenance shop due to wrong or missing spare parts , unplanned engine removal from an operator due to wrong or missing spare parts or added significant operator or engine maintenance shop risk , maintenance , cost , or workflow disruption . 4.4.1.3 Category 3 ( Minor Customer Impact ) A Category 3 escape is , but not limited to : a ) An issue that prevented the aircraft from achieving its intended mission but where safe operation was not jeopardized per CSOP – P20 ; b ) An issue where non-conforming material was dispositioned as not meeting the engineering design intent , with limitation to the product definition , has no effect on safe operation of engine and/or aircraft but resulted in precautionary OEM/field actions such as OEM interventions , engine removals , service information letter ( SIL ) per CSOP D-24 , special instruction ( SI ) per CSOP D-23 or service bulletin ( SB ) of compliance code of 4 and above , per CSOP D-29 definition ; c ) An issue where P & WC spare parts , services or other deliverable products that do not meet our customer contractual requirements and that are not

information letter ( SIL ) per CSOP D-24 , special instruction ( SI ) per CSOP D-23 or service bulletin ( SB ) of compliance code of 4 and above , per CSOP D-29 definition ; c ) An issue where P & WC spare parts , services or other deliverable products that do not meet our customer contractual requirements and that are not classified as significant customer escape . d ) A scheduled engine activity per CSOP P-20 ; P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03-04 Quality Management System REV : 7 Procedure DATE : 2 October 2023 PAGE : 8 of 13 Escape Management Process 4.4.1.4 Category 4 ( Internal Containment / No Customer Impact ) A Category 4 escape is , but not limited to an issue where non-conforming material was dispositioned as meeting the engineering design intent without limitation to the product definition , which has no impact on customer operations 4.4.2 Special Requirements : 4.4.2.1 High-Viz Note The purpose of a High-Viz note is to provide a consistent way for presenting material to Executive ensuring alignment across until full impact mitigation . A High Viz Note may need to be submitted by the Manager or delegate of the BU leading the escape to the senior management to provide a high level summary of the escape per QAI 08-03-04 . 4.4.2.2 PPAP For all parts , evaluating other potential failure modes of the process that generated the escape , defining and implementing the mitigation plan is mandatory for escape closure . 4.4.2.2.1Category 1 and 2 escapes If a PPAP package exists on the part , the review and resubmittal of the PPAP package with an update to the relevant PPAP elements ( reference QMSP 07-05-02 ) will be required as part of the Corrective & Preventive Action for escape closure . If an exemption is needed , the “ Significant Escape PPAP Exception Management ” Form 11705.docx must be submitted for approval with an explicit statement demonstrating how the requirements from section a ) were met . 4.4.2.2.2Category 3 escapes If a PPAP package exists on the part , the review and resubmittal of the PPAP package with an update to the relevant PPAP elements will be required as part of the Corrective & Preventative Action for escape closure . If an exemption is needed , concurrence of the exemption will be required from the respective QM and PQM with justification and recorded in the Escape Management Minutes ( EMM ) . 4.5 Escape Creation The accountable Manager or delegate of the organization that discovers the potential escape is responsible to initiate the communication through a potential escape meeting ( s ) . It is highly recommended to use the Escape Management Minutes to document the discussions and actions . If the issue meets the escape criteria as defined in 4.3 , the accountable organization will chair the following meetings , and will generate in PLM the EQP number , the Escape Management Minutes

to initiate the communication through a potential escape meeting ( s ) . It is highly recommended to use the Escape Management Minutes to document the discussions and actions . If the issue meets the escape criteria as defined in 4.3 , the accountable organization will chair the following meetings , and will generate in PLM the EQP number , the Escape Management Minutes , and all other relevant documentation . If the accountable organization is not clearly defined , a consensus is to be reached based on the available facts at the time of the potential escape meeting ( s ) to elect a responsible organization that will take the initial leadership and management of the Escape Management Process including all the required documentation . When the responsible organization is not easily identified based on the problem definition and information available , the Escape Council Team will identify the organization that will initiate the Escape Management P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03-04 Quality Management System REV : 7 Procedure DATE : 2 October 2023 PAGE : 9 of 13 Escape Management Process Process . The Escape Council is made up of , as a minimum , PQM , and Quality Managers from each of the Business Units involved in the escape . 4.6 Escape Ownership Transfer The ownership of the escape can be transferred following an ownership transfer meeting . Additional transfer meeting to be called if subsequent transfer is required . Prior to the transfer of an escape , the organization leading the escape must complete the following documentation in PLM : a ) Escape Management Minutes with all the information from the initial investigation findings b ) Relevant documentation supporting escape transfer c ) Escape slide , form 10686 , as applicable . Once the documentation is attached in the EQP folder in PLM , an official escape transfer meeting must occur between both organizations with the participation of Quality Performance to finalize the escape transfer to the accountable organization . 4.7 Red Flag Notice When required , to help address internal containment , Quality Performance may issue a Red Flag Notice ( RFN ) as per QMSP 08-03-04-01 . 4.8 EMM Exception Clause The EMM may be used to record the assessment in lieu of a BSQN when there is a possibility that a nonconforming part has been assembled into an engine and the actual condition of part can not be confirmed without disassembling the affected engine , provided both of the following conditions are met and agreed by Quality Performance , the applicable program DAA/CVE , and the Chief Engineering Office ( PI/A & C ) representative : a ) There is a low probability that the affected engine contains non-conforming part ; b ) Engineering assesses the non-conformity as acceptable as is . Evidence of compliance and agreement that this clause can be used must be documented within the EMM . If it is confirmed or likely that there are of

Chief Engineering Office ( PI/A & C ) representative : a ) There is a low probability that the affected engine contains non-conforming part ; b ) Engineering assesses the non-conformity as acceptable as is . Evidence of compliance and agreement that this clause can be used must be documented within the EMM . If it is confirmed or likely that there are of non-conforming parts within an assembled engine , parts would have to be dispositioned through a BSQN . 4.9 To address containment for product located outside P & WC production environment , Customer Service shall issue proper field documentation ( i.e. , an ICA such as an SB , SI , etc . or a Spares Parts Recall Letter ) as per CSOP P-14 . 4.10 Root Cause Analysis ( RCA ) 4.10.1 Escapes All escapes require a Root Cause Analysis to be completed . The RCA will be conducted as part of the EQP process , with the Escape leader responsible for the RCA coordination and the RCA will be included in the EMM . P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03-04 Quality Management System REV : 7 Procedure DATE : 2 October 2023 PAGE : 10 of 13 Escape Management Process 4.10.2 Escapes with SMS Folder Issues associated with SMS folders represent product safety concerns and require a root cause analysis ( RCA ) to identify potential process and systemic causal factors , beyond the “ initial ” proximate cause . This supports identification of appropriate corrective actions and potential read across scenarios . Not all SMS folders are associated with an escape ( EQP ) . If there is a P & WC escape ( EQP ) associated with the SMS Folder , the RCA will be conducted as part of the usual EQP process , with the Escape leader responsible for the RCA coordination , taking into consideration the team and approach outlined in SMSP 02-01-01 . 4.11 Escalation QP=No escapes which remain open for over a year will be reclassified as QP=Yes for visibility and tracking purposes only . The Escape Leader will continue to manage the escape as per the QP=No process . 4.12 The process for closure of an escape may be initiated once all questions of the EMM P & WC form 10254 have been addressed . 5 . The escape leader facilitates and coordinates with the responsible organisation to ensure that specific actions are addressed and closed . The manager of the origination is accountable to ensure that the appropriate steps are undertaken and addressed properly . Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03-07 Quality Management System REV : 8 Procedure DATE 6 November 2024 PAGE : 4 of 12 OEM Intervention Process 1 PURPOSE/SCOPE 1.1 To ensure OEM interventions on an engine , Nacelle parts , and EBU parts , are dealt with consistently by using a gated process , and are closed in a timely manner . 1.2 This procedure covers from the event at the OEM or in specific cases at intermediate facilities , to the final reporting of event closure back to the OEM by Product Quality Management . 1.3 This process is owned by Product Quality Management , who will also approve its access in Enovia . 2 DEFINITIONS / ACRONYMS 2.1 Definitions Customer Furnished Equipment ( CFE ) : EBU components that are provided by the customer for use in the assembly . Engine : The term engine implies engine and APU/APS . Engine Build Up ( EBU ) : Consist of components that are placed on the engine to functionally connect the engine to the aircraft . EBU components may consist of Customer Furnished Equipment ( CFE ) or of User Supplied Material ( USM ) . Experimental Engine : An engine that has been assembled tested and received an authorized release certificate as “ prototype ” status on a Transport Canada Civil Aviation ( TCCA ) / European Union Aviation Safety Agency ( EASA ) Form One by a P & WC Engine Centre experimental environment . Engine is not intended for upgrade to production and can not be used for service . Intermediate Facility : Facility that does not hold a production approval from their local civil authorities . Pre-Production Engine : An engine that has been assembled tested and received an authorized release certificate as “ prototype ” status on a TCCA / ( EASA ) Form One by a P & WC Engine Centre production environment and intended to be approved for in-service operation

Intermediate Facility : Facility that does not hold a production approval from their local civil authorities . Pre-Production Engine : An engine that has been assembled tested and received an authorized release certificate as “ prototype ” status on a TCCA / ( EASA ) Form One by a P & WC Engine Centre production environment and intended to be approved for in-service operation ; confirms that subject engine conforms to unapproved design data . Engine can not be used for in-service operation . Production Engine : An engine that has been assembled tested and received an authorized release certificate as “ new ” status on a TCCA / ( EASA ) Form One by a P & WC Engine Centre approved for the particular engine model ; confirms that subject engine conforms to approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation . Engine can therefore be used for in-service operation . OEM Intervention : An OEM Intervention must be raised to document visual or operation related non- conformities on the engine , engine parts , Nacelle parts , or EBU parts , e.g . scratches , exceedances , etc. , delivered at OEM facility or for any actions taken on an engine , engine parts , and shipped loose engine parts at intermediate facilities . An OEM Intervention must also be raised to document other events that need to be recorded for future reference , Interventions must be raised within the scope of QMSP 10-05 Recertification of work performed on engines after delivery by P & WC AMC 4-58 . There are 3 OEM Intervention type families , P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03-07 Quality Management System REV : 8 Procedure DATE 6 November 2024 PAGE : 5 of 12 OEM Intervention Process a ) Unscheduled interventions They are interventions related to unplanned actions required to report the above activities . These interventions are captured in P & WC quality performance metrics in order to elaborate preventive action plans with internal contributor teams . b ) Informational interventions They are interventions raised for an OEM liability or to record , following a trouble- shooting process completion , an acceptable condition for future reference . E.g . metal in oil , low oil pressure , shipping damage to box , etc. , which are corrected/disposition as per approved documentation . This information is useful for the contract admin team and Marketing to manage financial discussions with the OEM . c ) Scheduled interventions They are planned interventions that can be divided as ,  Scheduled OEM intervention This is an action performed on the Engine , engine parts , Nacelle parts , or EBU parts , following a formal request from the OEM or following an agreement between P & WC and OEM to perform work ( i.e . incorporate a product improvement , engine conversion , etc . ) ensuring no quality liability is assigned to P & WC .  Scheduled P

action performed on the Engine , engine parts , Nacelle parts , or EBU parts , following a formal request from the OEM or following an agreement between P & WC and OEM to perform work ( i.e . incorporate a product improvement , engine conversion , etc . ) ensuring no quality liability is assigned to P & WC .  Scheduled P & WC intervention at the OEM This is an action imposed by P & WC on the Engine , engine parts , Nacelle parts , or EBU parts , resulting from an escape per QMSP 08-03-04 and requires an EQP RFA reference ( Escape management tracking number added to the reference documentation of each OEMi ) from the responsible organization . The issue must be communicated in writing ( NOE , Disclosure letter , Memo ) prior to any action at the OEM . User Supply/Supplied Material ( USM ) : EBU components that are provided by P & WC for use in the assembly . This should include , but is not limited to : ground or flight operation , engine operating condition , details of the troubleshooting performed on a time-line basis , records for Engine/Part Hours and Cycles , etc . 5.1.3 The FSM is responsible for reviewing the ICA document ( EMM , SI or SB ) and P & WC specs ( CPW ’ s ) in order to define the troubleshooting and if a part/engine needs to be replaced . If support for the troubleshooting is needed , the FSM will contact via email a support organization at P & WC ( Engineering

The FSM is responsible for reviewing the ICA document ( EMM , SI or SB ) and P & WC specs ( CPW ’ s ) in order to define the troubleshooting and if a part/engine needs to be replaced . If support for the troubleshooting is needed , the FSM will contact via email a support organization at P & WC ( Engineering , Customer Service Engineering , Engine Center , CFirst , OPM , Marketing , etc. ) . CQM must be copied on all communication . 5.1.4 In case a part/engine condition can not be accepted or repaired as per an existent approved ICA document ( EMM , SI or SB ) and an engineering assessment is needed , an inspector from the engine center will raise a QN as per QMSP 08-03-02 , section 3 ( Requirements , for the Original equipment Manufacturing ) . 5.1.5 After QN is raised , CQM to communicate with Respective P & WC Organizations in order to get the highest priority set for the QN to support the part at the OEM . 5.1.6 Note : if during troubleshooting it is determined that the part/engine needs to be replaced , the QN is not needed . Note : For pre-production engines governed by an AQP a BI can also be used to perform work on the engine . 5.1.7 Engineering will communicate the QN disposition to the FSM , who will transfer it to the OEM using a previously agreed method of communication ( e-mail , internal customer ’ s system , etc. ) . 5.1.8 The FSM identifies the problematic part and provides a replacement ( if required ) from consignment inventory at the OEM . For parts that are not on consignment at the OEM , see section below “ Parts Returned ” . 5.2 Communication of the Event to P & WC 5.2.1 The FSM communicates all OEM interventions using an RFA ( PLM ) whether it is unscheduled , scheduled , or OEM liability . The RFA must contain all the information gathered during the intervention ( see 5.1 ) . It is the FSM ’ s responsibility to obtain all required information related to the event i.e . troubleshooting details , specific operating conditions , leak details , specific fault codes , and removed parts from the OEM . The OEM Intervention should be raised within 48 hours of the completion of the troubleshooting . The return number ( RMA ) will be provided to the FSM by DPM Cyient 5.4.3 When the parts are received at the OEM crib , the inspector will raise a QN as per QMSP 08-03-02 . 5.4.4 The QR will update existing QN or create new QN related to returned part and proceed with the investigation ( RTV , scrap at P & WC , send to Engineering or other consideration ) 5.4.5 If no return number is requested by the FSM then it is deemed that the FSM has verified that no investigation on the removed part is required by either P & WC or the OEM . The FSM will ensure that the part can not be upgraded . The FSM will then have the authorization to scrap the component locally . 5.4.6 Parts being scrapped locally shall be managed in accordance with OPMP 4-12 . 5.4.7 DPM Cyient will issue the return number for P & WC new consignment parts being returned to P & WC and record it in the RFA . 5.4.8 DPM Cyient will produce a weekly summary of all the parts not yet received at P & WC . This summary sheet will be produced on an OEM basis and distributed to the local FSM or back-up . 5.4.9 The local FSM is responsible for following up with the OEM until the parts are received at P & WC . 5.4.10 Once received at P & WC , the Distribution Centre/ Supply Management will handle the parts until they are returned to the group responsible for investigation . The QN number will be recorded in the RFA . 5.5 Investigation of the Event , Recommendation and Implementation of the RCCA 5.5.1 If there is a part returned to a Supplier , the Quality Engineers ( QE ) or Quality Specialist ( QS ) are responsible for ensuring the Supplier performs an investigation , and update the RFA accordingly . 5.5.1.1 When applicable the QE/ QS reviews and approves the Supplier RCCA . 5.5.2 If the liability is assigned to the Engine Centre , the Engine Manufacturing Centre Coordinator is responsible to ensure proper containment , and complete a RCCA . 5.5.3 If there is no part returned , whoever has ownership of the RFA is responsible for ensuring the RCCA is completed where applicable . 5.5.4 Reporting of significant events back to the OEM will be the responsibility of Marketing , Engineering or Quality as

Centre , the Engine Manufacturing Centre Coordinator is responsible to ensure proper containment , and complete a RCCA . 5.5.3 If there is no part returned , whoever has ownership of the RFA is responsible for ensuring the RCCA is completed where applicable . 5.5.4 Reporting of significant events back to the OEM will be the responsibility of Marketing , Engineering or Quality as mutually agreed . PURPOSE 1.1 To ensure that all nonconformities are identified , documented and processed to completion in an efficient manner and in compliance with the company , contractual and regulatory requirements . 1.2 This Quality Management System procedure ( QMSP ) and sub-sections applies to the Pratt & Whitney Canada ( P & WC ) Aircraft Powerplant Business ( APB ) as defined in the Quality Program Manual ( QPM ) including P & WC suppliers and partners to the extent specified in relevant Quality Plans . The application of these requirements also extends to product at OEM ’ s and facilities assembling engines into IPPS ( Integrated Power Plant System ) . 1.3 This QMSP and sub-sections define the requirements and responsibilities for the Control of Nonconforming Product . The process for the Control of Nonconforming Product consists of the following activities : a ) Identification b ) Documentation c ) Segregation or prevention from inadvertent use d ) Review e ) Disposition of nonconforming product ( including development material ) f ) Correction g ) Root cause corrective action 1.4 This procedure forms part of the Design Approval Procedures Manual ( DAPM ) and is subject to approval by Transport Canada Civil Aviation ( TCCA ) and fulfils the requirements for MRB and CAR 561 . 1.5 All activities related to receiving , creating , handling or transferring technical data in QMPS 08-03 series must comply to the QOP 1500-07-01-A ( Export Compliance Work Instructions ) . 2 . REQUIREMENTS 5.1 In no case will product be accepted or released if nonconformities adversely affect the airworthiness or the safety of the product . 5.2 Organizations ensure that all products which do not conform to product requirements are identified and controlled to prevent their unintended use or delivery . Procedures shall define the controls and related responsibilities and authorities for managing nonconforming product . 5.2.1 . Each organization , which manages nonconforming product , must establish adequate procedures and work instructions in accordance with these requirements . 5.2.2 . Each organization segregates potential nonconforming product and nonconforming product as follows : a ) When practicable , nonconforming product is physically placed in a designated quarantine area ; b ) When placing nonconforming product in a designated quarantine area is not practicable , nonconforming product is kept segregated and clearly identified ; c ) Movement of nonconforming product is restricted within the manufacturing unit where the nonconformity was found up to the completion of the subject traveler . All potential nonconforming product and nonconforming product is managed electronically by P & WC ERP per QMSP 08-03-02 . P & WC Proprietary Information – Subject to the restrictions on Page 1 PRINTED COPIES ARE CONSIDERED UNCONTROLLED QMSP : 08-03 Quality Management System REV : 11 Procedure DATE : 31 May 2023 PAGE : 6 of 7 Control of Nonconforming Product 5.2.3 Control of scrap material : 5.2.3.1 Each organization maintains a scrap control procedure which requires all product dispositioned scrap : a ) To be kept in a locked area and segregated from conforming parts ; b ) To have any acceptance/ identification marking cancelled ; c ) To be conspicuously and permanently ( where size permits ) marked as scrap , until it can be physically rendered useless for the original production purpose and it is not possible to restore the material to an airworthy application . This must be accomplished within 30 days after the scrap disposition , unless otherwise authorized by the applicable Quality Manager . 5.2.3.2 When the final scrapping of product is subcontracted and performed outside of P & WC premises , the following additional requirements apply : a )

original production purpose and it is not possible to restore the material to an airworthy application . This must be accomplished within 30 days after the scrap disposition , unless otherwise authorized by the applicable Quality Manager . 5.2.3.2 When the final scrapping of product is subcontracted and performed outside of P & WC premises , the following additional requirements apply : a ) The subcontractor must have a documented system for control , handling and disposal of P & WC scrap ; b ) The management representative with responsibility for quality at suppliers must approve the subcontractor disposing of scrap material ; c ) Product dispositioned scrap must be kept in a locked or sealed container from the time it leaves P & WC until it is destroyed at the subcontractor ; d ) Destruction of the shipments of PWC scrap must be witnessed by a representative of Operations Quality and attested on the subcontractor certificate of destruction ; e ) Evidence of scrap and disposal must be supplied by the subcontractor and maintained by the P & WC business entity ; 5.3 When nonconforming product is corrected it is subject to re-verification to demonstrate conformity to approved data . 5.4 Each sub-procedure of the QMSP 08-03 series defines the responsibility , review and authority for the disposition of nonconforming product and the process for approving personnel making these decisions . 5.5 Organizations managing nonconforming product use the following principals , where applicable as per QMSP 08-03-02 : a ) Taking action to eliminate the detected nonconformity ; b ) By authorizing its use by requesting release or acceptance by the Design Approval Appointee or Requesting to other authorized personnel as defined in the relevant procedure QMSP 08-03-02 ; c ) Obtaining customer concurrence when required by contract ; d ) Taking action to preclude original intended use or application as per QMSP 08-03-02 ; e ) Taking actions necessary to contain the effects of the nonconformity on other processes or products as per QMSP 08-03-02 if required . f ) For Partners , taking actions in accordance with the applicable Quality Plans ( QP ) / Collaboration Management Plan ( CMP ) 5.6 When nonconforming product is detected after delivery or use has started , each APB organization , as applicable , takes action appropriate to the effects , or potential effects , of the nonconformity . This is accomplished using the Escape Management process as per QMSP 08-03-04 or 08-03-01 as applicable . PURPOSE/SCOPE 1.1 . This procedure details requirements for release documentation for aircraft parts , spare parts , modules , engines or products . 1.2 . This procedure applies to all P & WC AMC organizations , including Engine Centres and Materials & Distribution ( Warehouse ) . 1.3 . P & WC may issue Authorized Release Certificates ( ARCs ) for new and prototype items produced under the 4-58 manufacturer certificate , including items produced by P & WC suppliers that are subject to P & WC ’ s Quality Assurance Program oversight , if the supplier has not provided their own ARC for the item . 1.4 . P & WC may also issue certificates for previously certified items ( i.e. , items received with a prior ARC issued by an appropriately approved supplier ) provided the component concerned is part of a higher assembly whose production is within the prime manufacturer certificate holder ’ s scope of approval e.g . a P & WC supplier issues a EASA Form One for a supplied part destined for a P & WC engine model , P & WC can issue a TCCA ARC for that part . 2 . DEFINITIONS/ACRONYMS 2.1 . Definitions : Note : The following definitions apply to Civil Aviation Requirements Aircraft The Aircraft Manufacturer herein referred to as the Original Equipment Manufacturer : Manufacturer ( OEM ) , integrates the engine into an aircraft airframe under a production approval issued by their relevant civil aviation authority Airtime : The time from the moment an aircraft leaves the surface until it comes into contact with the surface at the next point of landing . Airtime includes all time accumulated for an engine released on an ARC Form One as “ new ”

, integrates the engine into an aircraft airframe under a production approval issued by their relevant civil aviation authority Airtime : The time from the moment an aircraft leaves the surface until it comes into contact with the surface at the next point of landing . Airtime includes all time accumulated for an engine released on an ARC Form One as “ new ” and “ prototype ” . Airworthy : In respect of an aeronautical product , means in a fit and safe state for flight and in conformity with its type design . Airworthiness Instruction issued by the Minister that amends an approved document relating Directive : to an aeronautical product or that requires a modification , replacement or special inspection to ensure that an aeronautical product complies with approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation . Airworthiness A release of aeronautical product that conforms to TCCA Type Certification Release : and is in a condition for safe operation . Authorized The primary purpose of the ARC is to declare the airworthiness of new Release Certificate aeronautical products . It is used to certify new parts and engines and ( ARC ) provides a means for issuance of the statement of conformity required by Canadian Aviation Regulation Part V Subpart 61 ( CAR/STD 561 ) Appendix A. . The ARC ( P & WC Form 5720 ) is the official document for AMC 4-58 certification and release of aeronautical products . Bulk Shipment A quantity ( more than one ) of parts certified on one ARC and sent together . Certificate of Certificate issued in respect to an aircraft for which it conforms to a certified Airworthiness ( C of A ) : type design , and is in condition for safe operation Certified True Copy A copy ( usually a photocopy ) of an original document , that has on it an endorsement or certification that it is a true copy of the original document . Requirements for ARCs 4.3.1 The primary purpose of the ARC is to declare the airworthiness of aeronautical products . The ARC is not a delivery or shipping note . All entries on the ARC must be typewritten or clearly printed ( handwritten ) in block letters in permanent ink , and must be signed and stamped ( manually or electronically ) by a Company Authorized Signatory ( CAS ) approved and listed in QMSP 10-01-I . 4.3.1.1 When filling the ARC , all blocks must be completed . Non-applicable items should be either marked “ N/A ” or struck out . 4.3.1.2 When releasing engines or parts from an AMC under TCCA Approval 4-58 , the engine/part status in block 11 of the ARC must only be ‘ prototype ’ or ‘ new ’ . Non-applicable items should be either marked “ N/A ” or struck out . 4.3.1.2 When releasing engines or parts from an AMC under TCCA Approval 4-58 , the engine/part status in block 11 of the ARC must only be ‘ prototype ’ or ‘ new ’ . Block 13c of the ARC must indicate the P & WC TCCA Approval number i.e . ‘ 4-58 ’ , ‘ N/A ’ is not to be used . 4.3.1.3 When releasing engines or parts from an engine centre not yet approved by TCCA as an AMC under TCCA Approval 4-58 , Block 13c of the ARC must still indicate the P & WC TCCA Approval number i.e . ‘ 4-58 ’ . The engine/part status in block 11 of the ARC must only be ‘ prototype ’ . 4.3.1.4 For engines/parts to be eligible for ‘ new ’ status , Manufacturing Approval of the higher level engine model ( ref QMSP 10-06 ) is required . Nonapproved hardware , software , and instrumentation is not permitted on `` new `` status production engine . 4.3.2 Only Company Authorised Signatories ( CAS ) listed in QMSP 10-01-I are authorized and approved by P & WC to certify items on an ARC under P & WC TCCA Approval 4-58 . Only the Authorized Inspector stamp ( 149.01 ) or when applicable the Engineering Authorised Inspector stamp ( 15.03 ) and the Final Experimental Inspection and Program / Reprogramming of software stamp ( 15.02 ) may be used on an ARC . 4.3.3 Duly signed ARC ( ‘ new ’ status ) confirm that the products certified were produced in accordance with the policies and procedures identified in the Quality Program Manual ; conform to the applicable type design ; and are in condition for safe operation , subject to any conditions identified on the ARC . 4.3.4 Duly signed ARC ( ” prototype ” status ) confirm that the products certified conform to non-approved design data specified on the ARC . 4.3.5 A mixture of new and used items is not permitted on the same ARC . A mixture of ‘ new ’ ( conforms to approved data ) items and ‘ prototype ’ ( conforms to ‘ non-approved data ’ ) items is not permitted on the same ARC . Also applicable to virtual return Engines .  Section 5 , the control

delivered by P & WC 4-58 and subsequently returned to P & WC 4-58 AMC . 1.2 The following sections are defined :  Section 4 , the control of Engines returned from OEMs , P & WC FPS , P & WC LeaseCo , Operators , and Intermediate Facilities . DEFINITIONS/ACRONYMS 2.1 Definitions : Bulk Shipment – a quantity ( more than one ) of parts certified on one ARC and sent together . Certified true copy – is a copy of an original document , that has endorsement or certification that it is a true copy of the original document . It does not certify that the original document is genuine , only that it is a true copy of the original document . Certified True copies can be applied to ARCs or C of C ’ s . Intermediate Facilities - These are IPPS support/podding facilities where work may occur on P & WC engines . more of airtime are not eligible for support by AMC 4-58 . “ Prototype ” status engines , regardless of the airtime accumulated as “ Prototype ” status , are eligible for upgrade to “ New ” status by P & WC AMC 4-58 under an AQP . Following the upgrade to “ New ” status , if the engine has a total accumulated time of 50 hours or more of airtime , the engine will be considered ‟in-service″ and is no longer eligible for support by AMC 4-58 ( i.e. , can only be supported by AMO ) . Example : P-PW308C engine serial number CFXXXX initially released as “ Prototype ” on ARC Form One accumulated 45 hours of airtime under an Airworthiness Quality Plan ( AQP XXX ) . In accordance with said AQP , the engine was upgraded to “ New ” status , a new logbook and ARC referencing the initial “ prototype ” status ARC was issued by a P & WC CAS . The engine as “ New ” status accumulated 7 hours of airtime as part of aircraft production pass-off flight test . For the purposes of recording time , the engine has now accumulated a total of 52 hours of airtime making the engine ineligible for future support by P & WC AMC 4-58 . Engines with less than 50 hours of airtime may be returned without restriction . 4.2.2.1 Since accumulated airtime is not applicable to APUs , the accumulated runtime , either “ New ” or “ Prototype ” is used to determine eligibility for support by AMC 4-58 . 4.2.3 Engines delivered to Operator/end user  Engines delivered to Operators or end users are not eligible for support by P & WC AMC 4-58 because it is beyond the control /oversight of P & WC .  P & WC LeaseCo may return engines to P & WC AMC 4-58 if requirements of sections 4.2.1 , 4.2.2 and 4.2.4 are met . 4.2.4 Engine work certified by a Maintenance Organization Any work certified by Maintenance Organizations renders the engine ineligible for support by AMC 4-58 . A & P stamp must not be used as it is considered a Maintenance task . 4.3 Roles & Responsibilities 4.3.1 The P & WC FSM is responsible to :  Record in RFA system all relevant details on the reason ( s ) for the return of engine from OEM to P & WC AMC 4-58 .  Verify engine return eligibility in reference to sections 4.1 & 4.2 ; prior to engine return to the P & WC AMC 4-58 . PURPOSE / SCOPE 1.1 This procedure defines requirements for obtaining Manufacturing Approval ( MA ) from Transport Canada Civil Aviation ( TCCA ) or Quality Systems & Processes ( QS & P ) for the manufacture and release of new or existing production engines for civil aircraft applications . TCCA has issued a single Certificate of Approval number 4-58 to P & WC which includes several Approved Engine Manufacturing Centers , with each issued a “ site-specific ” Approval Limitation Record . This “ site-specific ” Approval Limitation Record lists the aeronautical products for which the identified engine center is approved to manufacture and certify . Upon request by P & WC and provided that the requirements of CAR/Standard 561 continue to be met , TCCA issues a new , in the case of an unapproved facility , or amends an existing , in the case of a currently Approved Engine Center , Approval Limitation Record . By doing so TCCA recognizes that the applicable Engine Center is equipped with adequate facilities and tooling , has access to all present and future design data , process specifications and other related information necessary for the continuing airworthiness of the aeronautical product , and that only appropriately trained individuals are assigned technical responsibilities with respect to the work performed on the engine or tasks and/or operations which the Approval Limitation Record authorizes . Subject to the provisions of this procedure , MA for which the Approval Limitation Record requires amendment shall be obtained from TCCA ; when the Approval Limitation Record does not require amendment by TCCA , MA may be granted by QS & P . TCCA at all times retains the right to provide oversight of the MA requested of QS & P . 1.2 MA from the Manager QS & P may be granted :  for new engine models derived from a TCCA-approved base model ( i.e . subject engine included on an existing Type Certificate ) already in production at the respective approved Engine Center i.e . included on the Approval Limitation Record .  when an engine is out of production for more than a two-year period . 1.3 This procedure applies to all P & WC AMC Engine Center facilities , and certain requirements also apply

( i.e . subject engine included on an existing Type Certificate ) already in production at the respective approved Engine Center i.e . included on the Approval Limitation Record .  when an engine is out of production for more than a two-year period . 1.3 This procedure applies to all P & WC AMC Engine Center facilities , and certain requirements also apply to organisations that support the MA process . 1.4 The MA Process represents the First Article Inspection process for AMC Engine Centers . The engine to be used for MA shall be a representative item from the first production run to verify that the production processes , documentation , tooling and personnel are capable of producing engines that meet production requirements . The engine to be used for MA shall have no unapproved hardware or software , or instrumentation installed , unless there is prior agreement with TCCA for engine configuration which includes unapproved hardware or software . Note : for non-civil engines , First Article Inspection is performed per contractual requirements . 1.5 Limited scope MA must be requested from TCCA . Each limited scope approval shall be detailed and controlled as per the applicable appendix . 1.6 QMSP 10-06-B and 10-06-E define the requirements which form the basis of the P & WC Approved Manufacture Centre ( AMC ) Quality Management Systems ( QMS ) with respect to aeronautical product manufactured by P & WC in conformity to type certificate data for which the design approval holder is not P & WC . SUB-CONTRACTING OF ENGINE CENTER ACTIVITIES 7.1 Approved P & WC Engine Centers may elect to sub-contract Engine Center activities to other P & WC Engine Centers or other P & WC-approved assembly facilities . 7.2 The scope of assembly work that may be sub-contracted is limited to sub-assemblies and modules only . 7.3 Engine test may only be sub-contracted to a TCCA-approved facility operated by P & WC . 7.4 The approved Engine Center is responsible to ensure that all sub-contracted work is performed in accordance with approved Engine Center procedures by qualified personnel using approved tooling and facilities . 7.5 Sub-contracting of any Engine Center activities must be approved by the Chief , Quality Discipline Engine Centers and the Manager , QS & P . In all cases , TCCA will be notified in advance of any Engine Center activity to be sub-contracted . TCCA may elect to perform oversight of this activity . 7.6 Sub-contracting of Engine Center activities is limited to 1st tier assembly facilities only i.e . the sub-contractor may not further sub-contract the work to another assembly facility . 8 . PURPOSE / SCOPE 1.1 . This procedure defines the requirements for the conversion of a production engine under CAR 561 - Manufacture of Aeronautical Products ( detailed in QMSP 10-00 ) from one build specification to another , or one engine model to another . This procedure does not apply to uncertified engines or engines installed on an aircraft with C of A or Export C of A . 2 . DEFINITIONS / ACRONYMS 2.1 . Definitions Production Engine : An engine that has received an Authorized Release Certificate confirming that subject engine conforms to approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation . Engine can therefore be used for service . Uncertified : An engine / part that has received an Authorized Release Certificate confirming that subject engine/part conforms to non-approved design data . 2.2 . Acronyms ARC Authorized Release Certificate C of A Certificate of Airworthiness CAR Canadian Aviation Regulation EC Engineering Change OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer AI Authorized Inspector OPM Operations Program Management P & WC Pratt & Whitney Canada QMSP Quality Management System Procedure SAP P & WC Enterprise Resource Planning System 3 . confirming that subject engine/part conforms to non-approved design data . 2.2 . GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 3.1 . An Engineering Change ( EC ) that contains instructions for the conversion of an engine , is required when the engine has been released with an Authorized Release Certificate ( ARC ) and has been delivered to the OEM / Customer except for `` Leasco Plant 5 '' . Operations Program Management ( OPM ) will ensure that applicable EC will be initiated . 3.2 . An EC for conversion is not required if the engine is still within P & WC Manufacturing premises ( i.e . not delivered to the OEM ) which includes but is not limited to the following scenarios : a ) When the engine is converted prior to the issuance of the Authorized Release Certificate ( ARC ) by the P & WC Authorized Inspector ( AI ) . b ) When an engine is converted from one build specification to another within the same engine model ( e.g . PT6A-42 to PT6A-42 different build specs ) . c ) When an engine is converted from one engine model to another ( e.g . PT6A-11AG to PT6A-21 ) after being shipped to Finished Parts Store or `` Leasco Plant 5 '' . Engine conversion requirements for engines returned from the OEM with no airtime , Engine Center is responsible to : a ) Retain the original ARC and the engine logbook in the engine history file . b ) Issue a new ARC and a new engine logbook per QMSP 10-02 ( no reference to the previous ARC or logbook is necessary ) . 3.4 .
